funFact: Bull sharks can survive in both salt and fresh water, and have been found more than 2,500 miles up the Amazon River — far from any ocean.
description: Bull sharks are among the most adaptable sharks on Earth, capable of osmoregulating between salt and fresh water. This brings them into frequent contact with humans in rivers and shallow coastal bays. They are considered one of the most dangerous sharks, not because they target people, but because of this habitat overlap.
habitat: Tropical and subtropical coastal waters, river mouths, estuaries, and freshwater river systems worldwide
geographicRangeDescription: Found in tropical and subtropical coastal waters on every continent except Antarctica. One of very few sharks capable of surviving in freshwater — individuals have been recorded hundreds of miles up the Amazon, Mississippi, Zambezi, and Ganges rivers. Common along the coastlines of the Americas, West Africa, the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and northern Australia.
diet: Fish, rays, sea turtles, dolphins, birds, and other sharks
size: Up to 3.4 m (11 ft); females larger

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 1–13 pups per litter; 10–11 month gestation; biennial cycle

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Fish, rays, sea turtles, dolphins, birds, and other sharks

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Low — biennial reproduction limits recovery speed

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Unique osmoregulation via kidneys and rectal gland; can survive in freshwater for years — the only large shark to do so

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and subtropical coastal seas worldwide; uniquely penetrates rivers and freshwater lakes far inland

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~150 m (492 ft); typically found in shallow coastal, estuarine, and riverine systems

populationTrendMeta.note: No direct census exists. Scale derived from exploitation reasoning: bull sharks are heavily fished across tropical coasts and rivers; a population of only ~100k could not sustain observed catch pressure without collapsing. The Near Threatened (not Endangered) IUCN status implies a population in the low millions. Values show ~52% decline since the 1980s, consistent with the NT/approaching-Vulnerable threshold.

preyDeclineRegions.name: SE Asia
preyDeclineRegions.note: Riverine and coastal prey severely depleted by artisanal and commercial overfishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: Africa (W. Coast)
preyDeclineRegions.note: Estuarine fish and ray stocks depleted by artisanal overfishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal fish and ray populations under heavy longline and net pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: Caribbean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef fish and coastal prey declining from overfishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. America
preyDeclineRegions.note: Freshwater fish stocks depleted by overfishing and riverine habitat loss

preyDeclineRegions.name: Australia
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal prey under moderate pressure; some managed fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: SE Asia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Heavy targeted fishing for meat and fins; dense artisanal gillnet networks in river mouths

fishingPressureRegions.name: Africa (W. Coast)
fishingPressureRegions.note: Gillnet and longline bycatch in coastal and estuarine fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
fishingPressureRegions.note: Coastal gillnet bycatch; targeted fishing near river mouths

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. America
fishingPressureRegions.note: River-based fishing pressure; some targeted take for local markets

fishingPressureRegions.name: Caribbean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Coastal bycatch; some targeted fishing in less regulated waters

fishingPressureRegions.name: Australia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Shark nets and drum lines; bycatch in managed coastal fisheries

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — declining across coastal and riverine ranges

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Degraded — estuarine nurseries impacted by dams, runoff, and development

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — generalist diet provides some resilience

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — targeted for meat and fins; heavy coastal bycatch

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Minimal — limited dedicated protections; some CITES coverage

threats.name: Targeted Fishing
threats.description: Heavily fished for meat, fins, and liver oil in tropical and subtropical markets. Shallow coastal and riverine habitat makes them easy to intercept with nets and longlines.

threats.name: Freshwater Habitat Loss
threats.description: Dams block river access to nursery areas, while agricultural runoff and urban pollution degrade water quality throughout their freshwater range. Bull sharks depend on low-salinity estuaries to give birth.

threats.name: Water Quality Degradation
threats.description: Chemical pollutants and heavy metals accumulate in coastal and riverine systems where bull sharks forage and pup, affecting juvenile survival and reproductive success.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Incidentally caught in coastal gillnets and longlines targeting other species. Coastal development that concentrates fishing effort near river mouths compounds this pressure.

actionItems.title: Advocate for River and Estuarine Habitat Protection
actionItems.description: Bull sharks need low-salinity estuaries and river systems to give birth and raise pups. Support policies that limit dam construction, reduce agricultural runoff, and protect mangrove and estuarine ecosystems.

actionItems.title: Support Shark Finning Bans and Enforcement
actionItems.description: Bull shark fins are commercially valuable and targeted across the tropics. Support national and international legislation banning the shark fin trade and pushing for genuine enforcement in SE Asia and West Africa.

actionItems.title: Advocate for Clean Water Legislation
actionItems.description: Chemical pollutants and heavy metals accumulate in coastal and riverine systems where bull sharks live and pup. Support clean water policy and corporate accountability for industrial discharge into coastal waterways.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Coastal gillnet and longline bycatch is a significant source of bull shark mortality. Use the Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch guide to avoid seafood caught with methods that incidentally kill bull sharks.

actionItems.title: Challenge Fear-Based Narratives
actionItems.description: Bull sharks' reputation as uniquely dangerous is largely a function of habitat overlap with humans, not inherent aggression. Sharing science-based information about bull shark ecology builds public support for their conservation.
